questions.question: What is the difference between var, let, and const in JavaScript?
options: var is function-scoped while let and const are block-scoped, and const cannot be reassigned
All three are block-scoped but const is immutable
let is global while var and const are local
const can be reassigned but let cannot
questions.correctAnswer: var is function-scoped while let and const are block-scoped, and const cannot be reassigned
questions.explanation: The var keyword is function-scoped and is hoisted with an initial value of undefined. The let and const keywords are block-scoped and exist in the temporal dead zone until declared. Variables declared with const cannot be reassigned, although the contents of objects and arrays declared with const can still be modified.

questions.question: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A function that has access to its own scope, the outer function’s scope, and the global scope
A function that only has access to global variables
A way to stop a function from executing
A method used to close event listeners
questions.correctAnswer: A function that has access to its own scope, the outer function’s scope, and the global scope
questions.explanation: A closure is created when a function remembers the variables from its lexical scope even after the outer function has finished executing. Closures are commonly used for data privacy, callbacks, and factory functions.

questions.question: What will happen if you try to access a variable before it is declared with let or const?
options: It will return undefined
It will throw a ReferenceError due to the temporal dead zone
It will automatically initialize to null
It will behave the same as var
questions.correctAnswer: It will throw a ReferenceError due to the temporal dead zone
questions.explanation: Variables declared with let and const are hoisted but cannot be accessed before their declaration. This period is called the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ), and attempting to access the variable during this time throws a ReferenceError.

questions.question: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
options: == compares both value and type, while === compares only value
=== compares both value and type, while == performs type coercion before comparison
There is no difference between them
== is deprecated in modern JavaScript
questions.correctAnswer: === compares both value and type, while == performs type coercion before comparison
questions.explanation: The strict equality operator (===) compares both value and data type without converting operands. The loose equality operator (==) performs type coercion before comparison, which can lead to unexpected results. Using === is generally considered best practice.

questions.question: How does the JavaScript event loop handle asynchronous operations?
options: It executes async code immediately before synchronous code
It pushes async callbacks to the call stack directly
It moves completed async tasks from the callback queue to the call stack when it is empty
It blocks execution until async tasks are finished
questions.correctAnswer: It moves completed async tasks from the callback queue to the call stack when it is empty
questions.explanation: JavaScript executes synchronous code first. Asynchronous operations are handled by browser or Node.js APIs, and once complete, their callbacks are placed in a queue. The event loop moves callbacks from the queue to the call stack only when the stack is empty.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the this keyword in JavaScript?
options: It always refers to the global object
It refers to the object that is currently executing the function
It refers to the parent function scope
It always refers to the DOM element
questions.correctAnswer: It refers to the object that is currently executing the function
questions.explanation: The value of this depends on how a function is called. In object methods, it usually refers to the object itself. In constructors, it refers to the newly created instance. Arrow functions do not have their own this and instead inherit it from the surrounding scope.

questions.question: What is the difference between null and undefined in JavaScript?
options: null is an assigned value representing no value, while undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned
undefined is an assigned value, while null means not declared
They are completely interchangeable
null is automatically assigned by JavaScript in all cases
questions.correctAnswer: null is an assigned value representing no value, while undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned
questions.explanation: Undefined is the default value for variables that have been declared but not initialized, as well as missing object properties and function parameters. Null is an intentional assignment that represents the absence of a value.

questions.question: What does Array.prototype.map do?
options: It modifies the original array directly
It creates a new array by applying a function to each element
It filters elements out of an array
It sorts the array in place
questions.correctAnswer: It creates a new array by applying a function to each element
questions.explanation: The map() method iterates over every element in an array, applies a callback function, and returns a new array containing the transformed values. It does not modify the original array.

questions.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code execution?
options: Synchronous code runs in parallel while asynchronous code runs sequentially
Synchronous code blocks execution until complete, while asynchronous code allows other operations to run before completion
Asynchronous code always runs faster than synchronous code
There is no difference in JavaScript
questions.correctAnswer: Synchronous code blocks execution until complete, while asynchronous code allows other operations to run before completion
questions.explanation: Synchronous code executes one statement at a time, blocking further execution until each operation completes. Asynchronous code allows long-running tasks, such as network requests or timers, to complete in the background while the program continues executing other code.

questions.question: What is the purpose of promises in JavaScript?
options: To replace all callbacks in JavaScript
To handle asynchronous operations and represent a future value or error
To make synchronous code run faster
To store data permanently in memory
questions.correctAnswer: To handle asynchronous operations and represent a future value or error
questions.explanation: A Promise represents the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. It can be in one of three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected. Promises improve readability compared to nested callbacks and work seamlessly with async/await.
